dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unconscious male at the Greyhound bus station in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Chris, an EMT with the rescue squad. What prompted you to call 911?
This is a call for an unconscious 46-year-old male. The bus driver found him slumped in a seat with no other passengers on board.
Staff, check if the patient is responsive to pain or verbal stimuli.
He doesn't open his eyes. There's minimal response when we apply pressure to one of his shoulders.
GCS is 7-eyes: 1, verbal: 2, motor: 3. He responds to pain only and is not alert.
Is the airway patent and clear of obstruction?
The airway is patent. No flaring or stridor noted. He maintains it in an upright position.
What is the breathing pattern-depth and rate-currently?
Equal bilateral chest rise and fall. Respiratory rate is 10. No cyanosis observed.
SpO2 is 90% on room air. I'll apply a non-rebreather mask for increased oxygen support.
What about peripheral pulses and skin condition?
Strong bilateral radial pulses. Skin is pale, warm, and dry. Capillary nail bed refill is less than 2 seconds. No edema observed.
Pulse is 98, regular.
Respiratory rate is 10, equal on both sides.
Blood pressure is 98/66.
Oxygen saturation is 90%. At room air.
Blood glucose is 120. No signs of hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.
Bystander, when did the bus driver find the patient unresponsive?
Just before the driver left the bus-approximately 11 PM.
What was the patient doing before becoming unresponsive?
He never showed unusual behavior. He just came with a small carry-on and was last seen exiting through the back door.
Were there any medications on him?
No medications found, but a small pink tube with white powder was in his mouth.
That might be substance use. Does he have any past medical history we need to know?
He mentioned having femoral artery surgery last week. He still has a wound.
Has he reported any allergies?
No known drug or food allergies.
I'll initiate four-lead EKG monitoring.
Performing CV-ECG-4 lead monitoring now.
The initial EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Let's begin 10 LPM oxygen via non-rebreather mask. Can someone assist in holding his head stable?
SpO2 increased to 94% with oxygen support. There's a slight improvement.
Pulse now reads 140, regular but fast.
I will now perform a 12-lead EKG for more detailed assessment.
Completed a 12-lead EKG. Interpretation is sinus tachycardia. No immediate electrical abnormalities noted.
As the patient regains some alertness, do you have any pain or chest discomfort?
No pain. I feel sleepy. I don't remember much from the past hours.
Prior to this, did you take any substances?
I used a mix of heroin and cocaine about 7 or 8 p.m. tonight.
Skin remains pale and dry with no rashes or obvious wounds.
Pupils are 3mm, sluggish at first but now reactive.
PERRL pupils, but slow reaction likely due to drug influence.
Administer naloxone (Narcan) 1mg intranasally for suspected opioid overdose.
SpO2 has increased to 100%. He follows commands now and appears more alert.
Second EKG completed-again, sinus rhythm. No acute changes.
We're following Protocol 3-15: Altered Mental Status due to opioid and stimulant abuse with femoral artery surgery history.
He may need a second dose of Narcan. I'll proceed.
Second dose of Narcan administered. Patient is opening his eyes on command. GCS is improving.
Patient is now oriented to person and time. SpO2 is stable at 94%.
Pulse remains elevated at 140 but no signs of pain.
Third 12-lead EKG shows sinus tachycardia with Mobitz I second-degree AV block, suggesting right ventricular hypertrophy.
Stabilizing for stretcher transport. Patient is alert, cooperating, and stable.
Ready for transport onto the stretcher. Secure all restraints during transit.
Begin electronic report to [COMPANY_NAME]. Informed them of drug exposure and abnormal EKG.
ED is aware of sinus tachycardia, Mobitz I, and possible right ventricular hypertrophy findings.
Also, a possible inferior infarct with occasional ventricular early complexes was observed.
Patient is now loaded onto the stretcher, secured for transport.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME]. ED is aware and awaiting us.
SpO2 remains at 94% on room air. No decline noted.
No obvious deterioration. Continuous oxygen and cardiac monitoring continuing.
Vital signs stable. Rechecking every 5 minutes. Blood pressure, glucose, and EKG unchanged.
Patient is now awake and communicative. All restraints remain secure.
Reiterating findings for [COMPANY_NAME]. They've been expecting us for handover in the trauma bay.
No issues during transport. The patient is now on a cot in the trauma bay.
All necessary reports are submitted. Care is now transferred to hospital staff.
Protocol completed. All documentation is filed.
